title: Madeleines
subtitle: Shell-shaped French sponge cakes with a signature bump on the back
excerpt: Small shell-shaped sponge cakes made from a beurre noisette batter rested in the fridge before baking. The thermal shock of cold batter meeting a hot tin and hot oven is what creates the characteristic hump on the back of a madeleine. Flavoured with lemon zest and vanilla. Eaten warm from the oven, ideally within an hour.
sourceNotes: The madeleine is a small French sponge cake baked in a shell-shaped tin, associated with the Lorraine region of northeastern France and with the town of Commercy in particular. The cake was popularised by Marcel Proust in À la recherche du temps perdu (1913), in which the narrator dips a madeleine into tea and is transported to childhood memory. The recipe has been part of French domestic baking since at least the 18th century. The characteristic hump is a sign of a well-made madeleine and requires the batter to be cold when it enters the oven.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The batter can be refrigerated for up to 24 hours. Madeleines must be baked and eaten fresh; they stale quickly.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. The hot tin and hot oven, combined with the cold batter, creates the hump. Preheat the tin in the oven for 5 minutes before filling.

recipeTools.notes: 12-hole madeleine tin, well buttered and dusted with flour

glossaryTerms.definition: The dome that forms on the back (flat side) of a madeleine during baking. It is caused by thermal shock: cold batter poured into a hot, buttered tin and placed in a hot oven. The outside of the batter sets rapidly while the inside is still liquid, and the continuing heat forces the liquid centre upward. Two conditions are required: the batter must be cold (rested for at least 1 hour) and the tin must be hot (preheated in the oven). A flat-backed madeleine indicates undercold batter or an underheated tin.

content.text: is the sign of a correctly made madeleine. Two things create it: cold batter and a hot tin. Make the batter and refrigerate it for at least an hour. Preheat the madeleine tin in the oven for 5 minutes before you fill it. Butter the tin while it is still cold, then put it back in the oven to preheat; the butter should sizzle faintly when you fill the shells.

attrs.prepNote: sifted, plus extra for the tin

content.text: Brown the butter in a small saucepan over medium heat until golden and nutty-smelling. Pour into a bowl and leave to cool until warm but no longer hot. Whisk the eggs, sugar, and honey together until pale and slightly thickened, about 3 minutes. Fold in the flour, baking powder, lemon zest, and vanilla. Fold in the warm brown butter. Cover the bowl and refrigerate for at least 1 hour, or overnight.

Heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Butter the madeleine tin well and dust with flour, tapping out the excess. Place in the oven for 5 minutes to heat the tin. Remove and spoon the cold batter into each shell, filling to just below the rim. Return to the oven and bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the edges are golden and the humps have formed on the back. Turn out onto a rack immediately. Eat warm.
